title: Resume where left off after `podman pull` fails due to missing space
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

While trying to pull a 12GB image, the storage space filled up completely after all the blobs were downloaded, but before the `podman pull` was complete; after the operation, all the downloaded blobs were removed and a subsequent `podman pull` had to restart the download. This is inconvenient and, given an operation might fail like this precisely when the images are larger and download times are longer, it might be better to keep the downloaded blobs until the operation is successful (image pulled) or to provide a flag to do so. 

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Have 15 GB free space
2. `podman pull` a 12 GB large image
3. download completes, but pull does not
4. when attempting the pull again, it downloads the image again

**Describe the results you received:**

Downloaded chunks were discarded, had to download the missing ones all over again.

**Describe the results you expected:**

I'd expected podman to keep the chunks and avoid downloading them over again (or, even better, resume interrupted downloads).

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

This happened to me once, since it was the only time I ran out of disk space while pulling an image.
I was trying to download unreal engine container image which are, in total, well over 15 GB in size. I had about 20GB of free space, downloaded and all the blobs could be downloaded successfully, but when `podman` failed to extract (?) the blobs, it removed the downloaded ones.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
$ podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.1.1
API Version:  4.1.1
Go Version:   go1.17.10
Built:        Tue Jan  1 01:00:00 1980
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

comments.body: @rhatdan WDYT? maybe a flag that tells podman to cache partial images temporarily? or a containers.conf field maybe to always do it
I leave these up to @vrothberg and @mtrmac
Not yet sure how to tackle this specific request.

We do now commit layers one at a time as they come, so in _some_ situations a blob should not need to be re-downloaded.

But I tend to think of even the _existence_ of the layer as an on-disk file as an implementation detail (we do that primarily so that we can validate the cryptographic digest of a layer before invoking a possibly-vulnerable decompression implementation, and we _could_ not do that, in principle), so exposing more tunables for persisting the temporary blobs is a bit weird. (Would users then also ask for the ability to point at a pre-populated directory, for some reason?)

Also, we have no range request / download resumption capability in the current design; so after downloading 11 out of 12 GB of a single layer, whatever we do, after the download fails, we are going to download all 12 GB again. So preserving the blobs would only help if the failure somehow hit the small window between downloading the blob and actually committing it (there might not even be a window at all, I don’t think we check for cancelation between finishing a download and committing a layer, but I haven’t checked in detail).

Basically, for a large image made from a many small layers, preserving or not preserving blobs should make no difference, most are going to be immediately committed. And for a large image made from a single huge layer which was not completely downloaded yet, preserving or not preserving blobs should make no difference _with the current implementation_, it’s going to be downloaded again.

---

Now, teaching the system about partial downloads and resuming at an offset is, in principle, possible (as long as the generic copy pipeline is not modifying the blob in any way, i.e. no encryption/decryption/compression/decompression/…).

---

(The flip side of the implied option not to remove local downloaded blobs that have not been committed into layers might be an option to remove committed layers if we don’t end up creating a complete image…)
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

> We do now commit layers one at a time as they come, so in _some_ situations a blob should not need to be re-downloaded.

I'm trying to pull big (~10GB) image from the ECR. podman-py timeouts every 1 hour or so and tried to download all chunks again (and times out).

podman cli pull behaves in the same way:
run `podman pull` 
wait for few blobs to be downloaded (`Copying blob a501ab2f6e26 done `)
kill pulling
start pulling again - all blobs are downloading again

The blobs are committed in order: if the first one isn’t fully downloaded yet, none of the later ones can be committed.

I’m afraid the code currently doesn’t do detailed enough debug logging to expose this; so, if the above doesn’t explain things, there would need to be extra code changes to understand the situation.

I am running into this problem while trying to download large pytorch images on a shaky DSL connection (Germany)

many of the blobs are very large (~7GiB) so blob-level progress can be an issue even if complete blobs are committed

is there perhaps a workaround using aria2 or similar to manually pull the blobs with multipart/resumption?

anyone find a work around for this terrible bug? it used my whole year data cap in the background pulling partial images and retrying.

I noticed a few requests for this seemingly basic feature...
https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/issues/14616

I mean, whatever happens, users will need to arrange to have enough disk space locally, to succeed. And once they do that, the behavior on running out of space … no longer matters as much?

Podman’s retry is fairly consciously trying not to retry on out-of-disk-space; if that fails, please file that as a separate failure. (And if the retry loop is not Podman’s, that’s not something Podman can fix.)

---

#14616 is _explicitly_ not about situations where users run out of space.
